lens.included_activities: For-profit circus, magic, ice-skating, traveling-carnival, and similar live-performance companies in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly provide produced shows to venues, promoters, corporate buyers, fairs, or other external customers under booking or performance contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Primarily self-promoted ticket-risk productions, grant-dependent nonprofit organizations, owner-only or non-transferable individual acts, promoters that do not produce their own shows, captive entertainment units, and companies whose economics depend mainly on venue ownership, intellectual-property licensing, or merchandise.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business bookings and fixed or negotiated performance fees from venues, promoters, fairs, casinos, resorts, and corporate-event buyers; some companies may also receive admissions participation or ancillary revenue.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines contract-performance businesses with consumer-facing ticket-risk productions, nonprofits, and personality-dependent acts. The lens is narrowed to recurring contracted show providers because those models have coherent external-service revenue and transferable operating organizations.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for NAICS 711100, not 711190 or the contracted-show lens. I grouped its occupation mix into digitally exposed administrative, marketing, finance, planning, design, and creative-preparation work versus low-exposure live performance, physical production, food service, and venue work, then weighted the groups approximately by employment and reported mean wages. Anthropic usage by broad SOC group informs which digital tasks are currently used with AI. The broader 711100 mix likely overstates exposure if theater administrative staffs are larger than circus and magic-company staffs, but it may understate exposure in lean touring companies where the same employee handles many digital tasks.
a.rationale: The broader industry employed 41.79% of workers in arts, design, entertainment, sports, and media occupations, while management, business and financial operations, and office support together accounted for another 23.68%. AI can materially assist marketing copy, customer outreach, scheduling, budgeting, bookkeeping, fundraising, design ideation, and production documentation, but the live physical performance and much of technical execution remain outside substitutable digital work. The range reflects uncertain 711190 occupation mix and the distinction between task assistance and avoidable labor.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census from 10 to 15 contracted circus, magic, ice-show, and traveling-production companies, mapped to O*NET tasks and current AI capability evaluations, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 711100 and includes theater, dance, and music companies outside this code.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers and does not identify whether tasks are already automated.
Observed Claude usage is not the same quantity as technical exposure or labor substitution.

rho.rationale: Over five years, standard tools should make marketing production, proposals, scheduling, bookkeeping, procurement support, and production documentation implementable in small touring companies. Implementation is constrained by fragmented systems, irregular project data, artistic quality control, safety-critical operations, union or contractor practices, and the need for human approval in customer and creative decisions. The estimate applies only to the exposed opportunity in a, not to live-performance labor generally.
rho.change_evidence: Time-and-motion studies before and after AI deployment at contracted touring-show operators, including error rates, customer acceptance, headcount avoided, and twelve-month persistence, would materially move the range.
caveats: No implementation study was found for NAICS 711190.
Lean organizations may adopt general-purpose tools quickly but lack clean data and dedicated change-management capacity.
Implementation must preserve safety, artistic quality, and contract compliance.

e.rationale: The official code covers companies producing live theatrical presentations such as circuses, magic shows, ice shows, and traveling carnivals. Some have repeat venue or event-buyer contracts and an operating organization independent of one performer, but many are ticket-risk, nonprofit, highly seasonal, owner-personality dependent, or primarily consumer-facing. The estimate is the share of the injected LMM-band firm count that fits the narrowed contracted-service and transferability lens.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the 25 injected LMM-band candidates, coding revenue by contract fees versus admissions, customer repeat rate, nonprofit status, owner dependence, and presence of a second management layer, would directly determine eligibility.
caveats: The supplied n is margin-bridged from firm counts and average receipts rather than observed EBITDA.
The most specific published revenue-model evidence found is from 2007 and does not isolate the current contracted subset.
Eligibility can change sharply with whether a show brand and performer relationships survive an owner exit.

s5.bridge: An SBA-hosted article reports a cross-industry survey in which 29% of business owners expected to exit within five years and 37% wanted a family transfer. I use 29% as an upper anchor, then discount exits for closures, internal or family succession, non-transferable owner-led acts, and transactions without control transfer. The generic exit statistic therefore likely overstates qualifying transfers for this lens, although sparse deal disclosure could hide some transfers.
s5.rationale: Qualifying transfer probability is below stated owner-exit intent because only external or otherwise qualifying control transfers of a continuing contracted-show operation count. Specialized creative identity, key-person dependence, seasonal volatility, and limited buyer depth reduce conversion of exit intent into transferable deals.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of DealStats, BizBuySell, Axial, PitchBook, and business-broker closed-transaction records tagged to circus, magic, ice-show, and touring live-production companies, paired with owner-age and succession interviews, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The source survey is old, cross-industry, and measures expected exit rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
Private small-company transfers and closures are poorly disclosed.
Internal family succession is not counted as a qualifying transfer under this primitive.

q.rationale: Fixed or negotiated performance-fee contracts allow implemented cost savings to be retained during the contract period, but venue and promoter buyers can capture part of the benefit at renewal through repricing. Competitive bidding, transparent production budgets, revenue-sharing clauses, and buyer concentration limit retention; differentiated show brands and switching costs support it. The discounted five-year range reflects partial rather than immediate pass-through.
q.change_evidence: A review of 50 current and renewed booking contracts showing fee structure, cost-plus provisions, admissions participation, renewal cadence, competitive rebids, and realized gross margin after automation would directly inform retention.
caveats: No current contract sample for the narrowed lens was available.
The source confirms admissions and contract fees as operating revenue categories but does not report current 711190 contract terms.
Customer concentration and show differentiation can move retention materially in either direction.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for the broader NAICS 711100 performing-arts-companies industry from $29.8 billion in 2024 to $36.9 billion in 2034, a 2.1% annual increase. Applying that constant-price annual rate for five years gives approximately 1.11. The broader industry projection may overstate 711190 if theater and music growth is stronger, while the NEA's rise in attendance at the survey's heterogeneous 'other live performances' category suggests potential offsetting strength for magic and circus-type demand.
d5.rationale: The base follows BLS real-output growth for the closest published industry aggregate. Live attendance remains meaningful, and NEA reported that attendance at 'other' live performances rose from 15% of adults in 2017 to 21% in 2022, but the category includes music genres outside 711190 and the period includes pandemic disruption. The wide range allows for discretionary-spending sensitivity, touring economics, event substitution, and renewed audience interest.
d5.change_evidence: Current constant-price booking volume, tickets sold, performance counts, and forward contracted dates from a panel of 711190 operators and major venue or fair buyers would materially move the estimate.
caveats: BLS output is for NAICS 711100 and not the narrowed contracted subset.
The NEA attendance category includes several music genres outside 711190 and measures people attending at least once, not quantity purchased.
Pandemic recovery distorts recent comparisons, and live entertainment is cyclical and discretionary.

o.rationale: The service basket is the production and delivery of a live theatrical presentation. Performers, production leaders, safety accountability, logistics, client coordination, and real-time execution remain operator-required even if AI handles substantial preparation and administration. Some marginal demand may shift to software-mediated or virtual entertainment, and buyers may self-produce simpler acts, but those effects are limited within the demand that remains after d5.
o.change_evidence: Venue-buyer surveys on self-production and virtual substitution, plus operator records of shows canceled or redesigned because software replaced the provider, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: No source directly measures operator displacement for 711190.
Rapid advances in synthetic media could increase substitution for some corporate or promotional events.
The estimate separates service elimination from AI assistance inside a continuing operator.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent target is a contracted touring-show operator rather than the entire residual performing-arts code. Such a company combines repeat venue or event-buyer relationships with a transferable production organization, but the eligible population is likely a minority because many firms are ticket-risk, nonprofit, seasonal, or inseparable from a featured owner-performer.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can reduce labor in proposals, marketing content, customer communication, tour and crew scheduling, budgeting, bookkeeping, procurement support, design ideation, and production documentation. It is much less able to substitute for physical performance, live technical execution, safety management, artistic direction, and accountable delivery at the venue, so the implementable opportunity is concentrated in overhead and preparation rather than the show itself.
narrative.value_capture: Negotiated or fixed performance fees should preserve some cost savings until renewal, while competitive rebids, buyer leverage, open-book budgets, and admissions-sharing can pass benefits back to customers. Brand differentiation, proprietary show concepts, and demonstrated reliability improve retention; undifferentiated event acts face faster repricing.
narrative.firm_availability: The injected LMM count is small and estimated rather than observed. After excluding nonprofits, ticket-risk productions, owner-only acts, and companies without repeat contract customers or transferable management, only a limited set is likely eligible. Generic small-business exit intent suggests some turnover, but actual qualifying transactions should be lower because closures and internal succession do not count.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS expects real output growth for the broader performing-arts-company industry, and NEA data show meaningful attendance in the heterogeneous other-live-performance category. Demand is nevertheless discretionary, cyclical, exposed to travel and venue economics, and vulnerable to audience-fashion shifts. For demand that persists, the physical and accountable nature of live production makes complete software displacement unlikely.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main evidence weakness is classification: the best workforce and forecast data cover all performing arts companies, while the target code itself mixes circuses, magic, ice shows, and traveling carnivals. Key-person creative dependence, performer retention, injury and insurance risk, seasonality, venue concentration, recession sensitivity, and weak disclosure of private transactions could all invalidate a firm-level thesis.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat contracted live-show delivery offers automatable administrative and production-preparation work while preserving a human-operated core service.
narrative.principal_weakness: The already-small estimated firm pool shrinks materially after excluding ticket-risk, nonprofit, and owner-dependent acts, and code-specific evidence is sparse.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 711190 Other Performing Arts Companies
sources.supports: Defines 711190 as companies or groups producing live theatrical presentations other than theater, dance, and musical companies, with circuses, magic shows, ice-skating companies, and traveling carnival shows as examples.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 711100 Performing Arts Companies
sources.supports: Reports 135,030 total jobs and occupation shares and wages, including 41.79% arts/design/entertainment/sports/media, 8.62% management, 7.58% business and financial operations, and 7.48% office and administrative support.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Economic primitives
sources.supports: Documents observed AI usage by O*NET and SOC task groups, increased arts and media writing use, and growing enterprise automation of office workflows such as email, document processing, CRM, and scheduling.

sources.title: 5 Reasons to Hire Your Child
sources.supports: Reports a cited survey in which 29% of business owners expected to exit within five years, 54% within ten years, and 37% wanted to transfer the business to family.

sources.title: Time and Money: Using Federal Data to Measure the Value of Performing Arts Activities
sources.supports: Uses Economic Census data to show other performing arts companies and explains that for-profit performing-arts revenue mainly came from admissions and contract fees; also documents material differences between taxable and tax-exempt organizations.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects NAICS 711100 real output from $29.8 billion in 2024 to $36.9 billion in 2034, a 2.1% compound annual increase, and employment from 142,100 to 147,200.

sources.title: Measuring the Arts: Indicator B.1, Who Attends Arts Events in Person?
sources.supports: Reports that 38% of U.S. adults attended a live arts performance in 2022 and that attendance at the survey's other-live-performance category, which may include magic and circus acts, rose from 15% in 2017 to 21% in 2022.
